questionText: Let $m$ and $n$ be integers. Consider the statements $P:\ mn$ is odd, and $Q:\ m$ and $n$ are both odd. Determine which of the symbols $\Rightarrow$, $\Leftarrow$ or $\Leftrightarrow$ correctly connects $P$ and $Q$, proving each direction that you claim.

steps.description: Write each statement in algebraic form.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ mn \text{ is odd}, \qquad Q:\ m \text{ is odd and } n \text{ is odd}
steps.explanation: Before comparing two statements we pin down exactly what each one asserts. Translating 'odd' into algebra later lets us test the claims by calculation rather than by feeling.

steps.description: Recognise that a biconditional needs two separate proofs.
steps.workingLatex: P \Leftrightarrow Q \ \text{ requires both } \ Q \Rightarrow P \ \text{ and } \ P \Rightarrow Q
steps.explanation: The two-way symbol $\Leftrightarrow$ is a pair of one-way claims bundled together, so we must establish each direction on its own. We prove the two arrows separately and only then combine them.

steps.description: Begin the reverse direction Q ⇒ P by writing both numbers as odd.
steps.explanation: Assume $Q$ is true and try to reach $P$. Every odd integer is one more than an even number, so the form $2a+1$ captures 'odd' exactly.

steps.description: Multiply the two odd numbers together.
steps.explanation: Expanding and then grouping every term except the final $1$ into a factor of $2$ shows the product has the shape 'even number plus one'. That shape is the definition of odd.

steps.workingLatex: mn = 2k + 1 \ \Rightarrow\ mn \text{ is odd}, \qquad \text{so } Q \Rightarrow P
steps.explanation: Since $2ab+a+b$ is an integer, $mn$ is genuinely of the form $2k+1$. Two odd factors therefore always give an odd product, so $Q$ forces $P$.

steps.description: Set up the forward direction P ⇒ Q using its contrapositive.
steps.explanation: Proving $P\Rightarrow Q$ directly is awkward, but a statement and its contrapositive are logically identical. It is far easier to assume a factor is even and watch the product become even.

steps.workingLatex: \neg Q:\ m \text{ is even} \ \text{ or } \ n \text{ is even}
steps.explanation: The opposite of 'both are odd' is 'at least one is even'. Because the situation is symmetric in $m$ and $n$, we may assume without loss of generality that $m$ is the even one.

steps.description: Use the even factor to compute the product.
steps.explanation: Writing the even factor as $2a$ pulls a factor of $2$ straight out of the product. A single even factor is enough to make the whole product even.
Since $an$ is an integer, $mn$ is a multiple of $2$, so $P$ fails. We have shown $\neg Q$ leads to $\neg P$.
Because a contrapositive is equivalent to the original implication, proving $\neg Q\Rightarrow\neg P$ has proved $P\Rightarrow Q$. So an odd product forces both factors to be odd.

steps.description: Combine both directions to choose the symbol.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{ and } \ Q \Rightarrow P \ \Rightarrow\ P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: Each statement forces the other, which is precisely what the two-way arrow means. The correct connective is therefore $\Leftrightarrow$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $P \Leftrightarrow Q$: the product $mn$ is odd if and only if $m$ and $n$ are both odd.
